coredata.dc:description: The growing demand for environmentally friendly hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production has fueled extensive research into synthesis strategy for photocatalysis though the use of the sunlight. However, the current challenge lies in the efficient production of photocatalysts without relying on sacrificial agents in the atmospheric environment. In this study, we propose a breakthrough approach to synthesize N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) by utilizing disulfide bonds in discarded shuttlecock waste and demonstrate its impressive performance (2062.4 µM g-1h−1) for photocatalytic H2O2 production. The significance of the S-O functional groups in N, S-CDs is not limited to the structural elements, since it is not only the key adsorption site for oxygen, but also active site to promote electron transfer from the bulk phase to surface of N, S-CDs during the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) process for photocatalytic H2O2 production. Furthermore, femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy (fs-TA) indicates that the presence of the S-O moiety is effective in prolonging the electron lifetime, which effectively inhibit the recombination of electron-holes, thus greatly improving carriers’ utilization. These findings provide new insight for the design of biomass carbon-based photocatalysts, which have important implications for the industrial application of solar energy for the synthesis of H2O2.

(g) EPR spectrum of N, S-CDs. (h) Schematic illustration for the formation of N, S-CDs during the hydrothermal reaction.

(a) Photocatalytic H2O2 production curves of N, S-CDs under different conditions. (b) A comparison of H2O2 generation performance between as-prepared N, S-CDs and other carbon-based photocatalysts in recent years. Photocatalytic H2O2 production under different (c) atmospheres, (d) pH values and (e) metal-ions. (f) Photocatalytic decomposition of H2O2 over N, S-CDs. (g) H2O2 formation (K f) and decomposition (K d) rate constant of without/with adding N, S-CDs. (h) Solar-to-chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency of N, S-CDs in water. (i) Stability tests of N, S-CDs for photocatalytic H2O2 production. (j) TEM image of used N, S-CDs. (k) EPR spectra of fresh and used N, S-CDs.

RRDE polarization curves over fresh and used N, S-CDs at 1600 rpm in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH with (a) ring current and (b) disk current. (c) H2O2 selectivity as a function of the applied potential. ESR signals of fresh and used N, S-CDs: (d) ·OH, (e) ·OO and (f) ·OOH. (g) Photocatalytic H2O2 production over fresh and used N, S-CDs under different scavengers. Current-potential curves of (h) fresh N, S-CDs and (i) used N, S-CDs before and after adding the electron donor (Ethanol, 10 v/v%) in saturated KCl solution under Xe lamp irradiation. Femtosecond transient absorption spectra of (j, k) fresh N, S-CDs and (m, n) used N, S-CDs. Time profiles of normalized transient absorption for (l) fresh N, S-CDs and (o) used N, S-CDs.

The change in the corresponding absorbance of −SOx groups in in-situ FT-IR spectra of (a) fresh and (b) used N, S-CDs with a periodic light irradiation for 14 min. (c) High-resolution XPS S 2p spectra of fresh and used N, S-CDs. (d) Proportion of the peaks representing each sulfur groups based on XPS results over fresh and used N, S-CDs. (e) Temperature programmed O2 desorption (TPD-O2) profiles of fresh and used N, S-CDs. (f) Schematic diagram of transition process of the oxygen vacancy in N, S-CDs during the photocatalytic H2O2 production.

head.ce:title: Efficient photocatalytic H2O2 production by using unstable S-O functional groups as oxygen adsorption and active sites in shuttlecock waste-derived N, S-doped carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing demand for environmentally friendly hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production has fueled extensive research into synthesis strategy for photocatalysis though the use of the sunlight. However, the current challenge lies in the efficient production of photocatalysts without relying on sacrificial agents in the atmospheric environment. In this study, we propose a breakthrough approach to synthesize N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) by utilizing disulfide bonds in discarded shuttlecock waste and demonstrate its impressive performance (2062.4 µM g-1h−1) for photocatalytic H2O2 production. The significance of the S-O functional groups in N, S-CDs is not limited to the structural elements, since it is not only the key adsorption site for oxygen, but also active site to promote electron transfer from the bulk phase to surface of N, S-CDs during the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) process for photocatalytic H2O2 production. Furthermore, femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy (fs-TA) indicates that the presence of the S-O moiety is effective in prolonging the electron lifetime, which effectively inhibit the recombination of electron-holes, thus greatly improving carriers’ utilization. These findings provide new insight for the design of biomass carbon-based photocatalysts, which have important implications for the industrial application of solar energy for the synthesis of H2O2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) stands as a high-value chemical with wide-ranging applications in wastewater treatment, chemical synthesis, liquid dyes, among others, attributable to its potent oxidizing properties [1]. Currently, the industrial-scale production of H2O2 mainly relies mainly on the anthraquinone method [2], a process notorious for producing the dangerous by-products and consuming large amounts of energy, requiring an environmentally friendly, low-energy and efficient method of H2O2 production to replace the conventional synthetic process. In ongoing studies, photocatalytic H2O2 production technology, especially H2O2 photosynthesis without sacrificial agents, is considered one of the green and sustainable methods because of the natural abundant water as feedstock and oxygen in the air, with sunlight as energy input [3].

In general, photocatalytic production of H2O2 can be achieved through three common mechanistic pathways: (i) oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) process [4]; (ii) water oxidation reaction (WOR) process [5]; (iii) a dual-channel pathway utilizing both ORR and WOR [6]. Consequently, the photocatalysts designed for H2O2 production were required to possess sufficient redox capability to be converted into the desired products from H2O and O2. In the past decades, tremendous effort has been contributed to the development of various photocatalysts for H2O2 production, such as metal oxides (ZnO [7], TiO2 [8], WO3 [9], BiVO4 [10], etc.), metal sulfides (ZnS [11], CdS [12], In2S3 [13], etc.), organic frames (metal organic frameworks, MOFs [14] and covalent organic frameworks, COFs [15]) and carbon-based materials (graphitic carbon nitride, g-C3N4 [16] and carbon dots, CDs [17]). Among them, CDs possess great potential as a novel type of carbon nanomaterials[18], displaying a wide variety of precursor sources [19], abundant functional groups [20], excellent stability [21], commendable optical absorption and photo-induced carriers’ generation [22], and adjustable energy band structures [23], soluble in water to form a homogeneous catalytic system [24], making them suitable for the photocatalytic H2O2 production. As previously reported, Zdražil et al. demonstrated the ability of Asp-CDs synthesized from aspartic acid to produce H2O2 in the presence of water and water/2-propanol mixtures under sunlight irradiation and verified the formation of long-lived holes on the CDs surface, accelerating H2O2 production through the WOR process [25]. Similarly, Gogoi et al. prepared the biomass-derived CDs dispersed in a polyurethane matrix can be used as high-efficient heterogeneous photocatalyst for H2O2 production through the green practice process, providing the benefits of green chemistry of availability, simple and sustainable preparation, catalyst stability, nontoxicity, recyclability and biodegradability [26]. Furthermore, Gu et al. also demonstrated the synthesis of functionalized CDs with anthraquinone (CDs-AQ), presenting the outstanding photocatalytic performance toward H2O2 evolution, and the obtained results revealed AQ in CDs-AQ composite acted as co-catalysts, promoting efficient carrier separation and accelerating the two-electron ORR pathway for solar-driven photocatalytic production of H2O2 [27]. Nevertheless, the low yield of photocatalytic production of H2O2 from CDs-based materials is fundamentally due to the fact that the redox capacity still needs to be strengthened. Previous rewards in the field of bioengineering have confirmed that disulfide bonds are the most common dynamic covalent bonds in proteins, which are sensitive to the redox environment in the organism [28,29], and the free sulfur atoms formed when disulfide bonds break could produce new disulfide bonds or sulfates during the redox process [30]. For example, Sun et al. exploited the apex drug loading in cancer cells, where the sulfur atoms of the disulfide bond can be oxidized to hydrophilic sulfoxide or sulphone to facilitate the release of oxidatively reactive drugs from the prodrug [31]. Tsutsuba et al. introduced disulfide bonds to prepare butylene dithiodiglycol succinate and showed that the prepared material remained stable under high environmental oxidation–reduction potential (ORP, an external stimulus in the natural environment) conditions, while the disulfide bonds were cleaved under low ORP environments and relied on this ability to achieve switchable biodegradation in aquatic environments, such as the ocean [32]. Inspired by this idea that feathers are rich in keratin, which is rich in disulfide bonds, we plan to develop the an efficient and stable H2O2-producing CDs material using discarded shuttlecocks as precursors.

In this work, we successfully constructed S-O functional groups in shuttlecock waste-derived N, S-doped CDs, which are based on the breakage of disulfide bonds in keratin, aiming to achieve efficient photocatalysis of H2O2. An unexpected finding emerged, demonstrating the ability of the surface-unstable S-O groups to transfer electrons from the bulk phase to O2 molecules that are adsorbed on the functional groups, meanwhile the process exhibited an extraordinarily high selectivity in the production of H2O2. Detailed in-situ Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analyses disclosed the possible mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production over as-prepared N, S-CDs. The implications of these findings extend beyond providing meaningful insights into the design of more intricate biomass carbon-based photocatalysts for providing the possibility of realizing large-scale industrial production of H2O2.

The Shuttlecock waste-derived N, S-CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method (Fig.S1). Typically, we choose goose feathers from the discarded feathers discarded in the badminton hall of Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, and then cut off the branches to get pure feathers. Then, 0.28 g of feathers and 30 mL of deionized water were added into the Teflon reactor and heated at 200 °C for different time (such as 10, 12, 14 and 16 h). After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was filtered with filter paper until the large particulate material from the solution was completely removed, giving a yellow transparent liquid as the N, S-CDs solution. When not specified, N, S-CDs are synthesized by a 14 h hydrothermal process. After freeze-drying, N, S-CDs solid powders were redispersed in aqueous solution at a concentration of 5 mg/mL.

Photocatalytic H2O2 production experiments were performed by irradiating simulated sunlight (AM 1.5G, 100 mW cm−2) to 40 mL N, S-CDs aqueous solution (5 mg/mL) in the air conditions. In the course of the experiment, 5 mL of N, S-CDs solution (5 mg/mL) was dispersed in 35 mL of deionized water, and the resulting dispersion was stirred vigorously in the dark for 30 min. Then, the dispersion was irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp (10 cm distance between the light source and the suspension surface, 100 mW/cm2 light intensity) under continuous stirring in the air condition. In order to investigate the trend of photocatalytic production of H2O2, 1 mL solution was taken every 20 min. The amount of H2O2 was analyzed by iodometry according to the previous research [33]. In general, adding 1 mL of potassium hydrogen phthalate (C8H5KO4) solution (0.1 M) and 1 mL of potassium iodide (KI) solution (0.4 M) to the above centrifuged solution, and staying 30 min. Since H2O2 can react with I- to convert I- to I3 -, the amount of I3 - can be tested at wavelength of 350 nm by UV–vis spectrophotometer, and the corresponding amount of generated H2O2 can be determined.

To investigate the decomposition behavior of H2O2 over the photocatalyst, 5 mL of the solution (5 mg/mL) was added to a 1 mM H2O2 solution (50 mL N, S-CDs), and the suspension was taken out and filtered every 20 min under visible light irradiation, the corresponding H2O2 concentration was studied based on the above method, the H2O2 decomposition rate was calculated by the following (Eq. (1)):

The initial step was the same as the photocatalytic H2O2 production, and when the test was ended, the reaction solution was recovered, and 1 mL of fresh N, S-CDs solution was added and placed overnight. Besides, deionized water was added to keep the total concentration of the solution constant.
In addition, the method for the determination of the electron transfer number during the production of H2O2 in this work is given in the Supporting Information .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and chemical compositions of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and particle size of as-prepared N, S-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 1 a, N, S-CDs exhibit the nearly spherical morphology with an average diameter of around 3 nm (Fig.S2). In addition, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis demonstrated that N, S-CDs show clear lattice fringes with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm [34], which is consistent with the (100) lattice plane of graphite carbon [35,36]. To further investigate the crystal features of N, S-CDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded in Fig.S3, presenting a sharp peak at around 21°, which is corresponded to the (120) crystal surface of carbon [37]. The functional groups of the surface over N, S-CDs were further analyzed by using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. In Fig. 1b, it can be seen that the peaks at 1120 and 1400 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of the C-S and C-N bonds [38], respectively, and the C-N bonds are in the form of graphite N and pyridinic N. Moreover, characteristic peak at 3130 cm−1 is ascribed to N–H bond[39], which can initially explain the appearance of pyrrolic N peaks. Furthermore, the characteristic absorption bands appeared at 1660 and 3430 cm−1 are corresponded to C = O and O–H, respectively [40], which indicates that the surface of N, S-CDs is enriched with hydrophilic groups (such as –COOH and –OH). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to analysis the composition of the obtained N, S-CDs. Fig. 1c shows the full-scan XPS spectrum of N, S-CDs, which is mainly composed of C, N, O and S elements. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1d) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.3, 284.7, 285.8 and 287.7 eV, which are assigned to C–C, C-N, C-O, and C = N/C = O bands, respectively [41–43]. In N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e), three kinds of binding energies at 399.0, 399.6 and 401 eV[44], corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [45,46]. Besides, the S 2p spectrum in Fig. 1f consists mainly of two peaks centered on 163.7 and 168.2 eV, indicating that sulfur atoms exist in both the thiophene-S and sulfate/sulfonate forms [47,48]. As we known, the main component of feathers is keratin, which is rich in cysteine and numerous disulfide bonds, mainly found in animal hair, feathers and hooves [49]. Next in importance, keratin proteins are particularly stable with high mechanical strength, with their particularly large disulfide bond content and crosslinking roles in the protein peptide chain [50], and must be treated with high temperature, high pressure, acid, base or enzymes to break. Since the reaction occurs in the autoclave, the oxygen content in the reaction solution is low, and the disulfide bond splitting is only combined with oxygen to form −SOx [51]. To confirm this, room temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was employed to reveal the presence of unpaired electrons in as-prepared N, S-CDs. From Fig. 1g, a distinct EPR signal appears at g = 2.002, the typical signal of unpaired electrons [52], demonstrating that the existence of −SOx in N, S-CDs, which were formed during hydrothermal process. Based on the above results, the schematic illustration for the formation of N, S-CDs during the hydrothermal reaction was proposed in Fig. 1h. According to the above tests, disulfide bonds in keratins can be cleaved at 200 °C under high pressure, causing the peptide chain therein to start to break also [53]. Free sulfur atoms formed after the disulfide break can bind to low concentrations of oxygen in the water, creating new sulfates, such as unsaturated SO2, −SO3 and saturated group −SO4, which may provide oxygen adsorption and reduction active sites in the N, S-CDs. Thermodynamically, the optical band structure determines whether the photocatalytic H2O2 generation reaction can proceed and the absorption region of the solar spectrum of the photocatalyst. To investigate the optical property of the N, S-CDs, UV–vis absorption analysis was carried out. In Fig.S4, it can be seen that a wide absorption range of around 200–400 nm can be found, and a strong characteristic peak at 246 nm in this range can be attributed to the π-π* transition within the carbon framework [54]. Combined Tacu equation curve (Fig.S5) of UV–vis spectrum [55] and Mott-Schottky (MS) plot in Fig.S6 [56], we know that the band positions of N, S-CDs (Fig.S7), which is thermodynamically possible to achieving photocatalytic H2O2 under light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic H2O2 production and stability tests
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic H2O2 production tests were carried out using simulated sunlight source (Xenon lamp 100 mW cm−2, 0.625 mg/mL catalyst solution, 40 mL mixed solution) of N, S-CDs solution dispersed in pure water under air atmosphere without adding any electron donor. From Fig. 2 a, it can be seen that in the absence of light or catalyst, there is no production of H2O2 during the 120 min reaction, whereas in the presence of N, S-CDs photocatalyst, H2O2 can be produced continuously with the increase of the irradiation time, proving that the reaction is indeed a photocatalytic reaction occurring by the binding of photons to water. Based on the standard H2O2 concentration curves in Fig.S8, as-prepared N, S-CDs photocatalyst exhibits a significant H2O2 production yield of around 103.12 µM. Usually, disulfide bonds in keratins can be easily destructed under high temperature, which relies on prolonging the heating time to increase the degree of decomposition [57]. Consequently, the effect of the hydrothermal reaction time during the synthesis of N, S-CDs photocatalysts on the photocatalytic H2O2 production was investigated. As can be seen in Fig.S9, the optimal photocatalytic activity of the N, S-CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal reaction for 14 h, which may be the optimal condition for forming the number of S-O active groups. In particular, the photocatalytic activity of H2O2 production for as-prepared N, S-CDs photocatalyst in this work was compared with other recent reports (Table S2) and the normalized results are given in Fig. 2b. The process of photocatalytic production of H2O2 is generally through water oxidation reaction (WOR) or oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) process. In order to reveal the process of H2O2 production of N, S-CDs system in water, different air conditions were employed and shown in Fig. 2c. The results exhibit that the reaction yield of photocatalytic H2O2 is dependent on the oxygen content in the reaction environment, independent of other gases, suggesting that H2O2 is formed primarily through the ORR pathway. Next, the effect of pH on the photocatalytic H2O2 production by N, S-CDs was investigated and exhibited in Fig. 2d, revealing that with the production of H2O2 decreased with the increasing pH values. As we know, if H2O2 is mainly due to the combination of ·OH radicals, then an increase in pH leads to a more efficient production of H2O2 since more OH– groups can react with h + to produce more ·OH [58]. However, the above results are opposite, which just indicates that N, S-CDs photocatalytic H2O2 production system in this work prefers the ORR process. Considering more applications, such as production H2O2 in industrial waste water or seawater, the capability of N, S-CDs to photoproduced H2O2 in different anions/cations environment was tested (Fig. 2e), which has little impact on the production of H2O2, indicating that N, S-CDs photocatalyst shows great potential. It is worth noting that H2O2 decomposes readily in the presence of light, thus the H2O2 decomposition behavior of N, S-CDs solution was investigated (Fig. 2f) and corresponding the formation rate constant (Kf) and decomposition rate constant (Kd) of H2O2 over N, S-CDs photocatalyst were calculated and given in Fig. 2g. In dark, the Kd and Kf constants of the N, S-CDs were 22.59 and 0 µM h−1, respectively, while during the light irradiation, the corresponding Kd and Kf values of this system were 9.32 and 51.05 µM h−1, which further favors the generation of H2O2 in the photocatalytic reaction. Moreover, the solar chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency of N, S-CDs in the reaction system was calculated, and the maximum value of the SCC was 0.096 % (Fig. 2h). Stability is also an important indicator of the application of photocatalysts, so the circulating photocatalytic H2O2 reactions over N, S-CDs were carried out (Fig. 2i). Most unexpectedly, the H2O2 yield showed a significant decrease after the first cycle, with a difference of 2.89 times compared with the initial cycle, while it remained stable in the subsequent tests, which prompted us to further explore the causes leading to this particular phenomenon. Usually, the failure of the catalytic activity of the CDs may be associated with its easy agglomeration [59]. In order to verify whether agglomeration of N, S-CDs occurred, TEM image of N, S-CDs after photocatalysis was recorded and provided in Fig. 2j, and it exhibits that the size and morphology were not changed, indicating the reunion phenomenon has not occurred in as-prepared used N, S-CDs. Furthermore, we observed the solution color change of the used N, S-CDs after reaction. In Fig.S10, the color of the used N, S-CDs solution changed from pale yellow to brown, which led us to guess whether the active sites of N, S-CDs would be oxidized by the H2O2 produced by themselves. Consequently, EPR was again used to detect changes in lone unpaired electrons in the −SOx groups of used N, S-CDs. As provided in Fig. 2k, the results show that the unpaired electrons of the used N, S-CDs indicate changes in the unsaturated −SOx groups on the surface of N, S- CDs due to the H2O2-oxidation self-produced, which profile also indicates that unsaturated −SOx groups of N, S-CDs are the reactive active site of photocatalytic H2O2 production. The above tests showed that the presence of the unstable-SOx group increases the photocatalytic activity of N, S-CDs, and this group changes the color of N, S-CDs due to oxidation. It is therefore necessary to characterize the changes of N, S-CDs before and after the reaction to determine whether their properties have changed or what changes have occurred. After the −SOx groups were oxidized, the N, S-CDs absorption edge is red-shifted from 430 to 456 nm (Fig.S11), and the band gap is narrower than for fresh N, S-CDs (Fig.S12). Afterwards, the CB potential of used N, S-CDs was calculated based on MS plots (Fig.S13), and the corresponding energy band structure diagrams of used N, S-CDs was given in Fig.S14, demonstrating that oxidation of −SOx groups mainly affect kinetic processes rather than thermodynamic processes. It is illustrated in Fig.S15 by FT-IR that the carbon framework structure of N, S-CDs is stable after the reaction, but the slight changes occur belong to the surface S-O groups [60]. Extensive literature confirms that the unique fluorescence properties of CDs may originate from the carbon core or unpaired electrons on the surface [61]. Consequently, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of fresh and used N, S-CDs were recorded and presented in Fig.S16, demonstrating a significant change in the fluorescence properties of the used N, S-CDs. The maximum emission of fresh and used N, S-CDs also changed from 380 to 360 nm, while the emission intensity underwent a substantial decrease, which effectively indicates that the disappearance of the unpaired electrons in the S-O groups on the surface of the N, S-CDs led to the decrease in fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization efficiency of the photo-generated charges over fresh and used N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The number of transferred electrons and selectivity during photocatalytic H2O2 generation over fresh and used N, S-CDs were investigated by electrochemical measuring technology based on the rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE). In general, the disk current intensity (Id) and ring current (Ir) of RRDE arise from the oxidation reaction of H2O2 and the reduction reaction of oxygen, respectively [62]. As depicted in Fig. 3 a and b, compared with used N, S-CDs, Ir is lower and Id is higher for the fresh N, S-CDs, which indicates that the presence of unstable S-O groups in N, S-CDs could enhance reaction kinetics for the disassembly and synthesis of H2O2. Furthermore, the number of electrons transferred by fresh and used N, S-CDs were calculated as 1.5 and 1.8, according to the formulae, indicating that the change of S-O groups not significantly affect the two-electron ORR pathway (Fig.S17). Meanwhile, Fig. 3c illustrates fresh N, S-CDs with containing unpaired electrons of S-O functional groups possess the excellent selectivity of O2 into the reduction of H2O2. To further validate the H2O2 generation process, the active species in N, S-CDs photocatalysis were determined by the electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis. From Fig. 3d, e and f, under the same conditions, the used N, S-CDs exhibited a rather weak *OH signals [63] in comparison of fresh one, whereas intensities for *OOH and *O2 – signals [64] were similar to fresh N, S-CDs, suggesting that the changes in the surface S-O groups have not alter the two-electron pathway. In general, *OH radicals come from two reaction process: (i) oxidation reaction (WOR) process of photo-generated holes (h+ ) [65]; (ii) combination of produced *O2 – with H+ [66,67]. Thus, in order to investigate whether *OH originates from the WOR, trapping experiments on N, S-CDs were carried out by using AgNO3 and ethanol as trapping scavengers for electrons (e-) and h+ (Fig. 3g). After removing the effect of h+ , the H2O2 production of fresh N, S-CDs decreased significantly, which suggests that the generation of H2O2 over fresh N, S-CDs is mainly achieved by the ORR pathway, while the WOR pathway possesses a low contribution. Notably, the yield of used N, S-CDs was elevated, indicating that the photo-induced carriers (e- and h+) of used N, S-CDs are susceptible to recombination. Whereas, after the addition of AgNO3, it can be shown that the photo-generated e- of fresh and used N, S-CDs play a major role in the production of H2O2, and the rate of decomposition of fresh N, S-CDs is much higher than the rate of h + production, so that H2O2 production cannot be detected in fresh N, S-CDs. Not only that, the photocurrent tests with the addition of ethanol over fresh and used N, S-CDs (Fig. 3h and i) also illustrates this view: it is apparent that the photocurrent intensity changes in the used N, S-CDs, but not in fresh N, S-CDs. The promotion of the photocatalytic activity of H2O2 by the unstable S-O groups in N, S-CDs is a kinetic enhancement process, thus the femtosecond transient absorption (fs-TA) spectroscopy was employed. Under the excitation of a 325 nm pump laser, the fs-TA spectra of fresh and used N, S-CDs were in the 450–800 nm region for one and in the 500–900 nm region for the other (Fig. 3j and m). The fs-TA intensity decreased significantly with longer probe time, indicating a reduced active photo-induced charges (Fig. 3k and n). After fitted kinetic decay curves by two-exponential function (Fig. 3i and o), the observed slow decay component T1 (178.75 and 130.47 ps) over fresh and used N, S-CDs reveals the trapping of electrons from CB into trap states, while component T2 (20.017 and 4.486 ps) of fresh and used N, S-CDs is corresponding to the fast recombination of excited electrons and holes [68], which suggests that the fast electron-hole combination was indeed suppressed through the S-O groups of fresh N, S-CDs and effectively provides more direct evidence for kinetically enhanced H2O2 production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transition process of S-O groups in N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further verify the effect of the unpaired electrons of the unstable S-O groups on the H2O2 production process, the in-situ Fourier transform infrared (in-situ FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed to explore the changes in the molecular structure of fresh and used N, S-CDs during light irradiation. In Fig. 4 a and b, the characteristic peaks of the intermediates (·HOOH, ·OOH and ·O2 –) during the photocatalytic H2O2 production were found in fresh and used N, S-CDs. Notably, a significant increase in the broad peak representing S = O bonds in the range of 1000–1150 cm−1 in the fresh N, S-CDs but not in the used one, demonstrating a significant change of −SOx groups in fresh N, S-CDs during the reaction. And according to elemental analyses, the content of the elements C and N remained almost unchanged (Fig.S18 and Table S2), thus the S-element of fresh and used N, S-CDs underwent a more detailed comparative analysis. For deconvoluted S 2p spectra in Fig. 4c, the peak intensities representing −SOx of used N, S-CDs appeared significantly elevated compared to fresh N, S-CDs, and the proportions of −SO2, −SO3, and −SO4 in the peak regions of the −SOx groups were listed in Fig. 4d. The results show that the ratio of −SO2 decreases sharply after the reaction, while the ratio of--SO3 increases accordingly, also suggesting that −SO2 is an unstable S-O groups with significantly reduced photocatalytic H2O2 activity after oxidation. Meanwhile, H2O2 decomposition experiments with used N, S-CDs also illustrate this view (Fig.S19). In addition, temperature-programmed desorption O2 (TPD-O2) was also recorded for fresh and used N, S-CDs, and an obvious gap in oxygen adsorption capacity after the oxidation of −SO2 can be observed in Fig. 4e, thus it was discovered that the unstable −SO2 in N, S-CDs can also act as an active adsorption center for oxygen. According to the above experimental results, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production over N, S-CDs is provided in Fig. 4f. In the initial stage, fresh N, S-CDs with −SOx groups can be excited by the light irradiation to generate photo-induced carriers (e - and h +). Next, the e - could migrate from the bulk phase to the −SO2 groups on the surface of the N, S-CDs and further combine with the adsorbed oxygen to achieve the formation of H2O2 via the ORR process. In a low H2O2 production concentration environment, the −SO2 groups on the surface of the N, S-CDs is far from being oxidized by self-produced H2O2, which can continue and efficiently photocatalytic H2O2 production. Meanwhile, the h + also undergoes a lesser WOR process, reducing the meaningless photo-generated charge combination. After a certain period of irradiation, the concentration of H2O2 gradually increased, which then leads to the oxidation of from −SO2 to −SO3 groups in used N, S-CDs. As the −SO2 groups are oxidized in the high H2O2 production concentration environment, the molecular structure of N, S-CDs becomes stable, only on the edge of S atoms attack of pyrrole N as the active site of H2O2 [45], whereas −SO3 groups formed in N, S-CDs by H2O2 oxidation can still be used as the center of adsorption of oxygen, but with a lesser capacity of adsorption than that of −SO2, resulting in the drastically reduced generation of photocatalytic H2O2.

In summary, we firstly report a metal-free biomass-based N, S-CDs photocatalyst for efficient H2O2 production without any sacrificial reagents and co-catalysts in an atmospheric atmosphere. A series of characterization results indicated broken keratin disulfide bonds in feathers during hydrothermal processes, followed by the formation of unstable −SO2 functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs, which can not only serve as active sites to effectively transfer electrons from the bulk phase to the surface of N, S-CDs, but also as an oxygen adsorption center for H2O2 production during the ORR process. As a result, the production rate of H2O2 over as-prepared fresh N, S-CDs photocatalyst reaches 2062.4 µM g-1h−1, which is larger than that previously reported carbon-based photocatalysts until now. However, the superficial −SO2 groups N, S-CDs of is readily oxidized by the high concentration of H2O2 produced by itself, leading to a decrease in its activity, which also prompted us to think about how to stabilize these groups of N, S-CDs during the photocatalytic H2O2 production reaction. This work provides meaningful ideas to design biomass carbon-based photocatalysts for green and low-cost H2O2 production, which is momentous for the industrial application of solar energy.
